content.full_text:
State of California

Memorandum

To : Opinion Request Meeting
    Participants

Date : Sept. 12, 1977

From : FAIR POLITICAL PRACTICES COMMISSION
      Ted Prim

Subject: Questions by Bill Schwartz

The following questions were asked by Bill Schwartz of
the San Diego County Counsel's office.

1.  Aside from any fees he might receive,
does an official incur any reporting obliga-
tions by virtue of acting as a conservator for
a relative's estate?

2.  If a bank located outside the jurisdiction
holds a trust deed for a local official, is
the bank doing business in the jurisdiction by
virtue of making loans to people who live
inside the jurisdiction?

3.  If a county agency retains an attorney to
draft a conflict of interest code, is the
attorney a consultant? If so, when would such
a person have to be included in the conflict
of interest code?


TP:plh

Conclusions:

1)  Conservator, like trustee, is not the owner and therefore
need not report the holdings of the conservatorship or trust.

2)  Pending the Baty opinion, the minimum contacts approach to
jurisdiction is the appropriate standard.

3)  The attorney in this case is not a consultant and need not
be included in the code.

TP:jo

California

Fair Political Practices Commission
P.O. BOX 307 · SACRAMENTO 91304 · · · 1100 X STREET BUILDING, SACRAMENTO, 9581

June 27, 1977

James D. Simon
Staff Attorney
Department of Benefit Payments
744 P Street
Sacramento, California 95814

Dear Mr. Simon:

This is in response to your letter of 31 May requesting
informal advice concerning whether under the Department's
Conflict of Interest Code designated employees are required
to report as an "investment" or a "source of income", contributions to the State of California Deferred Compensation Plan
for employees.

Our advice is that they need not be reported. Having
analyzed the descriptive materials of the plan, we cannot
conclude that any of the various insurance plans are
"investments". Article X of the plan clearly states:

"The State shall have the sole ownership of all
investments made pursuant to this Plan and no
participant shall have any interests therein or
the right to acquire the same."

Furthermore, Government Code §82034 excludes "any insurance
policy" and seems to require an ownership interest of some
nature in order to constitute an "investment". Similarly,
§82030 which defines "income" excludes from that definition
both "salary from a state agency [§82030(b)(2)] and "any
insurance policy" and interest or deposits in financial
institutions and shares in a credit union [§2030(b)(7)]. We
think the deferred salary plan, taken as a whole, is "salary
from a state agency" within the meaning of §82030(b)(2). We
believe that the policy enunciated in its formal opinion No. 76-
074, Richard J. Moore, supports this conclusion. We do not
believe that reporting (and potential disqualification) by
designated employees of their participation in this plan is

Simon
27, 1977
age two

intended or necessary under the Political Reform Act.

Thank you for writing. I trust this answers your question.
If I can be of further assistance, please feel free to call on
me at 2-6444.

Sincerely,

Kenneth W. Goshorn
Research Specialist III
Conflicts of Interest Division

KWG:mia
Attachment

State of California

Fair Political Practices Commission
P.O. BOX 807 • SACRAMENTO, 95804 • 1100 K STREET BUILDING, SACRAMENTO 9541

August 23, 1977

Mr. Vincent V. Mackenzie
Chief Counsel
Energy Resources Conservation
and Development Commission
1111 Howe Avenue
Sacramento, California 95825

Re: Opinion Request No. 77-012

Dear Mr. Mackenzie:

This letter is to further clarify our response to your
referenced request for an opinion. By way of a letter dated
June 1, 1977, our Executive Director, Mr. Michael Bennett,
denied your request but issued informal advice to resolve
the matter. That advice mainly centered on the question of
whether a designated employee's investment in a mutual fund
would create any reporting or disqualification requirements
concerning utilities in turn held by the fund. We concluded
that absent a situation where the designated employee's holding exceeded a 10% interest in the total assets of the fund,
no such obligations existed. That advice was a proper inter-
pretation of the Political Reform Act of 1974 and should
stand.

However, it has since come to our attention that, in all
likelihood, the interest of the employees you described in
your request was, in fact, premised on the employees' parti-
cipation in the State of California Deferred Compensation
Plan. While our previous advice was based on the stipulation
that the employees had "investments" (see Government Code
§82034) in the "fund", to the extent the interest was
acquired pursuant to the Deferred Compensation Plan, that
interest would not constitute an investment or income to
the employee within the meaning of §§82034 or 82030. The
attached letter addressed to Mr. James Simon of the Department of Benefit Payments articulates the Commission's inter-
pretation on this subject.

cent V. MacKenzie
August 23, 1977                                                Page Two


     This subsequent analysis, of course, does not alter
the reasoning or result of our previous advice.

     If you have any questions or if I can be of further
assistance, please call me at 322-6444.

                                                  Sincerely,

                                                  Kenneth W. Goshorn
                                                  Kenneth W. Goshorn
                                                  Research Specialist III
                                                  Conflicts of Interest Division


KWG:glb

Request No. 77 012

Date Received 4/29/77

STATE OF CALIFORNIA—THE RESOURCES AGENCY

Response Due 5/14/77

ENERGY RESOURCES CONSERVATION
AND DEVELOPMENT COMMISSION
1111 HOWE AVENUE
SACRAMENTO, CALIFORNIA 95825

April 25, 1977

Daniel H. Lowenstein, Chairman
Fair Political Practices Commission
1100 K Street
Sacramento, California 95814

Dear Chairman Lowenstein:

The Energy Resources Conservation and Development Commission
has directed me to request a formal opinion from the Fair
Political Practices Commission on the question set forth
below. This request is made pursuant to Government Code
section 83114 and Title 2, section 18320 of the California
Administrative Code.

As you know, the F.P.P.C. recently approved this Commission's
Conflict of Interest Code. Nevertheless, certain issues
remain unsettled, and it is the purpose of this request for
an opinion to resolve one such major uncertainty. The
factual circumstances described below potentially apply to
the Commissioners and numerous employees of the Energy
Commission.

Facts Presented:

A representative of the Energy Commission makes or participates
in the making of governmental decisions (as those terms are
utilized in Government Code § 87100) that bear on the actions
and financial posture of California utilities, including
powerplant siting determinations and utility conservation
programs. This same individual maintains significant interests
(over $1,000) in both the T. Rowe Price New Income Fund, Inc.
and the T. Rowe Price Growth Stock Fund, Inc., mutual funds that
are available to state employees through the state employee
deferred compensation program. Both mutual funds hold interests
in the same California utilities that are subject to regulation
under the Energy Commission's statutory mandate.

Daniel H. Lowenstein                    -2-                    April 25, 1977


Question Presented:

Does the Energy Commission representative's investment in
either the T. Rowe Price New Income Fund, Inc. or the T. Rowe
Price Growth Stock Fund, Inc., given the factual circumstances
posited above, give rise to a conflict of interest under
Government Code section 87100? Specifically, is it reasonably
foreseeable that the individual's involvement in the decision-
making process will have a material financial effect, distinguish-
able from its effect on the public generally, on those invest-
ments? Note: for purposes of this question, participation in
either of the funds is considered to be an "investment" as
that term is used in Government Code section 87103. This
conclusion is based in part upon informal representations of
F.P.P.C. staff.

Prompt resolution of this issue is requested. I recognize,
however, the far-reaching significance of an interpretation.
Should you or your staff have any questions regarding this
matter, please contact Richard Frank of this Office at
telephone number 322-4926.


Sincerely,

[Signature]
Vincent V. MacKenzie
Chief Counsel


VVM:RF:mg
cc: Commissioners, ERCDC
    Executive Director, ERCDC

sections.question:
Does the Energy Commission representative's investment in either the T. Rowe Price New Income Fund, Inc. or the T. Rowe Price Growth Stock Fund, Inc., given the factual circumstances posited above, give rise to a conflict of interest under Government Code section 87100? Specifically, is it reasonably foreseeable that the individual's involvement in the decision-making process will have a material financial effect, distinguishable from its effect on the public generally, on those investments? Note: for purposes of this question, participation in either of the funds is considered to be an "investment" as that term is used in Government Code section 87103. This conclusion is based in part upon informal representations of F.P.P.C. staff.

Prompt resolution of this issue is requested. I recognize, however, the far-reaching significance of an interpretation. Should you or your staff have any questions regarding this matter, please contact Richard Frank of this Office at telephone number 322-4926.
sections.conclusion:
1) Conservator, like trustee, is not the owner and therefore need not report the holdings of the conservatorship or trust.
2) Pending the Baty opinion, the minimum contacts approach to jurisdiction is the appropriate standard.
3) The attorney in this case is not a consultant and need not be included in the code.

June 27, 1977

James D. Simon  
Staff Attorney  
Department of Benefit Payments  
744 P Street  
Sacramento, California 95814

Dear Mr. Simon:

This is in response to your letter of 31 May requesting informal advice concerning whether under the Department's Conflict of Interest Code designated employees are required to report as an "investment" or a "source of income", contributions to the State of California Deferred Compensation Plan for employees.

Our advice is that they need not be reported. Having analyzed the descriptive materials of the plan, we cannot conclude that any of the various insurance plans are "investments". Article X of the plan clearly states:

"The State shall have the sole ownership of all investments made pursuant to this Plan and no participant shall have any interests therein or the right to acquire the same."

Furthermore, Government Code §82034 excludes "any insurance policy" and seems to require an ownership interest of some nature in order to constitute an "investment". Similarly, §82030 which defines "income" excludes from that definition both "salary from a state agency [§82030(b)(2)] and "any insurance policy" and interest or deposits in financial institutions and shares in a credit union [§82030(b)(7)]. We think the deferred salary plan, taken as a whole, is "salary from a state agency" within the meaning of §82030(b)(2). We believe that the policy enunciated in its formal opinion No. 76-074, Richard J. Moore, supports this conclusion. We do not believe that reporting (and potential disqualification) by designated employees of their participation in this plan is

intended or necessary under the Political Reform Act.

Thank you for writing. I trust this answers your question. If I can be of further assistance, please feel free to call on me at 2-6444.

embedding.qa_text:
QUESTION: Does the Energy Commission representative's investment in either the T. Rowe Price New Income Fund, Inc. or the T. Rowe Price Growth Stock Fund, Inc., given the factual circumstances posited above, give rise to a conflict of interest under Government Code section 87100? Specifically, is it reasonably foreseeable that the individual's involvement in the decision-making process will have a material financial effect, distinguishable from its effect on the public generally, on those investments? Note: for purposes of this question, participation in either of the funds is considered to be an "investment" as that term is used in Government Code section 87103. This conclusion is based in part upon informal representations of F.P.P.C. staff.

Prompt resolution of this issue is requested. I recognize, however, the far-reaching significance of an interpretation. Should you or your staff have any questions regarding this matter, please contact Richard Frank of this Office at telephone number 322-4926.

CONCLUSION: 1) Conservator, like trustee, is not the owner and therefore need not report the holdings of the conservatorship or trust.
2) Pending the Baty opinion, the minimum contacts approach to jurisdiction is the appropriate standard.
3) The attorney in this case is not a consultant and need not be included in the code.

June 27, 1977

James D. Simon  
Staff Attorney  
Department of Benefit Payments  
744 P Street  
Sacramento, California 95814

Dear Mr. Simon:

This is in response to your letter of 31 May requesting informal advice concerning whether under the Department's Conflict of Interest Code designated employees are required to report as an "investment" or a "source of income", contributions to the State of California Deferred Compensation Plan for employees.

Our advice is that they need not be reported. Having analyzed the descriptive materials of the plan, we cannot conclude that any of the various insurance plans are "investments". Article X of the plan clearly states:

"The State shall have the sole ownership of all investments made pursuant to this Plan and no participant shall have any interests therein or the right to acquire the same."

Furthermore, Government Code §82034 excludes "any insurance policy" and seems to require an ownership interest of some nature in order to constitute an "investment". Similarly, §82030 which defines "income" excludes from that definition both "salary from a state agency [§82030(b)(2)] and "any insurance policy" and interest or deposits in financial institutions and shares in a credit union [§82030(b)(7)]. We think the deferred salary plan, taken as a whole, is "salary from a state agency" within the meaning of §82030(b)(2). We believe that the policy enunciated in its formal opinion No. 76-074, Richard J. Moore, supports this conclusion. We do not believe that reporting (and potential disqualification) by designated employees of their participation in this plan is

intended or necessary under the Political Reform Act.

Thank you for writing. I trust this answers your question. If I can be of further assistance, please feel free to call on me at 2-6444.
